prompt: Choice B: Skeletal muscle hyperplasia—the generation of new muscle fibers—is not a recognized physiologic response to training in adult humans. Most strength gains and muscle growth in resistance training come from hypertrophy, where the size of individual fibers increases. Satellite cells do become activated in response to mechanical load and help support hypertrophy by donating nuclei, but they do not cause new fiber formation. While animal models may demonstrate some hyperplasia, human muscle fibers respond to training primarily by getting larger, not by increasing in number. Given this, the histologic finding of hyperplasia is not expected in this athlete. Choice D: These are hallmark changes seen in endurance-trained muscle. With prolonged aerobic activity, the body adapts by enhancing oxygen delivery and usage—achieved through capillary angiogenesis and increased mitochondrial biogenesis. These changes allow slow-twitch (type I) fibers to sustain activity over time with greater efficiency. However, this athlete’s training is anaerobic in nature, prioritizing strength and power rather than oxidative endurance. Type II (fast-twitch) fibers dominate such activity, and they do not adapt by increasing mitochondria or capillaries.

prompt: Choice A: Cartilage fibrillation with pannus formation is a hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), an autoimmune disease in which chronic synovial inflammation leads to the formation of pannus — a vascular granulation tissue composed of fibroblasts, myofibroblasts, and inflammatory cells. The pannus invades cartilage and bone, causing erosions and deformities. RA typically presents with prolonged (>1 hour) morning stiffness, symmetric involvement of small joints (MCP, PIP), and systemic symptoms such as fatigue or anemia of chronic disease. Laboratory tests often reveal elevated ESR/CRP, rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Patients often present with weakness in mid-range abduction and numbness over the lateral deltoid area ("regimental badge" region). This patient has no trauma history, no sensory deficits, and is specifically unable to initiate abduction, making deltoid weakness unlikely. Additionally, the pain elicited with the empty-can test points to supraspinatus pathology, not deltoid dysfunction. Choice B: Bicipital tendinopathy typically presents with anterior shoulder pain, worsened by resisted elbow flexion or forearm supination. The Yergason and Speed tests are used to evaluate the long head of the biceps tendon. This patient, however, reports pain with abduction, not flexion or supination. His symptoms are more consistent with rotator cuff involvement rather than biceps pathology. The location of pain and inability to abduct the arm further supports supraspinatus injury over bicipital tendinopathy. Choice C: Glenohumeral joint arthritis presents with progressive, diffuse shoulder stiffness and pain, often seen in elderly patients with limited both active and passive range of motion. Crepitus and joint deformity may also be present. In contrast, this patient is relatively young, has preserved passive motion, and the pain is activity-related and focal. These features are not characteristic of joint degeneration. The acute onset and overhead activity–related mechanism also argue against arthritis.
answer: Bicipital tendinopathy causing anterior shoulder pain with initiating abduction and a positive empty-can test—align more closely with supraspinatus involvement. Infraspinatus dysfunction would be tested with resisted external rotation, which was not indicated here.

prompt: one would expect perifascicular atrophy and perivascular inflammation, not basophilic rimmed vacuoles. The progression of dermatomyositis is usually more subacute, not slowly progressive over years. following is the most likely diagnosis? Choice C: Lambert-Eaton syndrome typically presents with proximal muscle weakness, especially in the hip and shoulder girdles, and often improves with repeated use (facilitation). It is also commonly associated with autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth or erectile dysfunction, none of which are present here. Furthermore, Lambert-Eaton is most often associated with paraneoplastic syndromes, especially small cell lung cancer. There is no mention of underlying malignancy or autonomic findings in this case. Muscle biopsy is typically normal or nonspecific in LEMS — not notable for rimmed vacuoles. Choice D: Polymyositis typically presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, such as difficulty rising from a chair or combing hair, but distal muscles are usually spared. It typically affects middle-aged adults, and patients may have elevated creatine kinase and autoantibodies. The weakness progresses over weeks to months and tends to respond to immunosuppressive therapy. On biopsy, one would expect endomysial mononuclear inflammation with CD8+ T cells, but not rimmed vacuoles. The presence of distal involvement and basophilic vacuoles in an older man makes polymyositis less likely. Choice E: Statin-induced necrotizing myopathy often presents with rapid onset of muscle weakness and markedly elevated CK levels in patients who are taking statins. This case lacks mention of statin use, and labs are reported to be normal, including CK. The histologic hallmark of this condition is muscle fiber necrosis with minimal inflammation, not rimmed vacuoles. Additionally, this type of myopathy progresses more quickly and may be associated with anti-HMGCR antibodies. The patient’s slow progression over two years and unique biopsy findings are not consistent with this diagnosis.

prompt: Choice B: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) is a chronic inflammatory condition typically presenting with joint swelling, morning stiffness, and decreased range of motion. It often affects multiple joints and may be accompanied by systemic symptoms such as fever, rash, or fatigue. In this case, the patient has no morning stiffness, no joint effusion, no systemic symptoms, and his joint exam shows full range of motion. The pain is also activity-dependent, which is more typical of mechanical or overuse injuries than inflammatory arthritis. JIA would not present with isolated pain localized to the tibial tuberosity without joint involvement. Choice D: A patellar tendon rupture usually occurs following acute trauma and presents with sudden pain, swelling, inability to extend the knee, and a high-riding patella (patella alta). The patient typically cannot bear weight or actively straighten the leg. In contrast, this case describes a gradual onset of pain without trauma, full range of motion, and continued ability to participate in sports. There is no mention of a popping sensation, functional loss, or patellar displacement. The clinical picture clearly lacks the severity and acute presentation seen in tendon rupture.
